{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_2","type":"agent_message","text":"Confirmed with:

```bash
node packages/cli/dist/index.js scan evals/fixtures/05-stress-ia-drift --format json
```

The scan reports both `crime_00011` (`action_label_drift`) and `crime_00012` (`copy_ia_drift`) for the same delete-action alias group: `archive`, `delete`, `remove` across `SeatList.tsx`, `TeamList.tsx`, and `UserList.tsx`.

Canonical label set I’d use:

- Primary destructive action: `Delete`
- Confirmation title/button: `Delete {item}`
- Non-destructive unlinking only: `Remove`
- Reversible state transition only: `Archive`

For this fixture, the evidence says these are being used for the same destructive action, so I’d align all three components on `Delete` and avoid `Remove` / `Archive` unless the underlying behavior is genuinely different.

I’d refactor `src/components/SeatList.tsx` first. Both findings point at it as the primary file, and it currently participates in two drift aliases: `archive` and `remove`. Fixing `SeatList.tsx` first should collapse the noisiest local inconsistency, then `TeamList.tsx` should change `Archive` to `Delete`, and `UserList.tsx` should change `Remove` to `Delete`.

Detector difference on this fixture:

`action_label_drift` is the more specific finding: it is about UI action verbs for the same action group. This is the one I’d cite for changing button/menu labels in `UserList.tsx`, `TeamList.tsx`, and `SeatList.tsx`.

`copy_ia_drift` is broader: it treats the same alias spread as copy / information architecture drift. On this fixture it produces the same evidence, but the implication is wider: inconsistent product vocabulary can mislead agents and reviewers about whether these are separate flows or the same operation."}}
